lastPrompt: i have a couple of linear issues in both the ops project and also in the equity project - do any of these address this gap?

content.text:
Good question to ask — and the answer is pointed. **One issue was supposed to fix exactly this and is marked Done; the other is adjacent but explicitly won't help.**

## ABA-132 — the direct hit, but it's closed and the gap survived it

[ABA-132 "Recalibrate /stock-signal GARP thresholds per profit_stage × ai_layer"](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-132) **(Done, 2026-05-17)** is the issue that targets this gap by name. Its problem statement:

> *Premium-multiple quality compounders (**ASML monopoly**, NFLX pricing power, ADYEN.AS European moat)… the market correctly pays a premium for durability that PEG can't see. **Result: 5 of 7 COVERAGE.md tickers get model_ready=NO for structural rather than informational reasons.***

And its acceptance criterion:

> *All 7 COVERAGE.md tickers produce **model_ready=YES** (or a model_ready=NO with a **qualitatively-defensible reason, not "PEG > 2 mechanically"**).*

That criterion is **violated today.** ASML came back `model_ready=NO` for a *mechanical* reason — the 8% CAGR floor (used when `eps_growth_5y` is null) divided into a clean fwd P/E of 45.5 → PEG 5.69 > the INFRASTRUCTURE CAUTION band of 4.0. ABA-132 recalibrated the *bands* (that's where INFRASTRUCTURE WATCH≤4.0 came from), but it didn't fix the *PEG input* for the case where there's no forward consensus and the multiple is high. So this is best treated as a **regression against a closed issue**, not new greenfield work — ABA-132 should reopen, or a new bug should be filed citing it.

## ABA-67 — adjacent, names ASML, but explicitly out of scope for the gate

[ABA-67 "bookings/backlog fetch for INFRASTRUCTURE capital equipment"](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-67) **(Todo, High)** is all about giving ASML a real forward-growth signal (net bookings / EUV mix / High-NA units from the 6-K) instead of a floored guess. That's the *right* long-term source for the number the floor is faking. **But it disqualifies itself from this gap explicitly:**

> *Scope boundary: … **Does not affect /stock:signal verdicts.** The TAM ceiling risk flag in /stock:signal already captures the structural concern; this ticket is about **growth rate inputs only**.*

So ABA-67 feeds the *model's* DCF growth rate — it would never run for ASML, because the block happens upstream at the *signal* gate, before `/stock-model` is ever reached. It doesn't unblock the gate.

## Everything else — no
ABA-65 (segment revenue), ABA-68/69 (timing: revision counts, earnings date), ABA-103/133 (WACC auto-derive), ABA-95, ABA-113 — none touch the signal PEG-floor gate.

## Bottom line
No **open** issue will fix this as written. The gap is squarely a **failure of ABA-132's acceptance criteria for ASML** — the recalibration covered the bands but left the floored-CAGR-into-high-multiple path producing a mechanical CAUTION. The clean fix is one of:
1. Reopen ABA-132 (or file a bug referencing it) to raise/replace the 8% floor for INFRASTRUCTURE names with no `eps_growth_5y` — e.g. profile-keyed floor, or fall back to revenue CAGR.
2. Let ABA-67 land *and* have it emit a signal-side growth input (a scope expansion ABA-67 currently forbids).

Want me to draft the bug — framed as an ABA-132 regression with the ASML repro (PEG 5.69, floor 8%, clean fwd P/E 45.5) — in the Ops project, and link it to ABA-132 and ABA-67? I won't write to Linear without your go-ahead.

input.title: /stock-signal blocks /stock-model for ASML — 8% CAGR floor × high multiple yields mechanical CAUTION (ABA-132 regression)
input.description:
`bug` · regression against the closed [ABA-132](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-132) acceptance criteria · surfaced 2026-06-18 while testing ABA-125 end-to-end across the seven covered tickers.

## Symptom

`/stock-signal ASML` returns **CAUTION → MODEL_READY: NO**, so `/stock-model ASML` correctly refuses (gate works as designed). The result is that **ASML cannot be modelled through the pipeline at all** — the one COVERAGE.md ticker that can't produce a DCF.

## Repro (2026-06-18, live yfinance)

```
ASML — ESTABLISHED — INFRASTRUCTURE
  eps_ttm:           29.94 EUR
  ntm_eps:           41.578 EUR        (get_estimates)
  eps_growth_5y:     null              ← no long-term consensus on yf
  sbc/share:         0.525 EUR         (clean fwd EPS = 41.05)
  clean fwd P/E:     45.5
  raw NTM/TTM CAGR:  7.2%  → floored to 8%  (eps_growth_5y null)
  PEG = 45.5 / 8 = 5.69
  INFRASTRUCTURE band: BUY ≤2.0 / WATCH ≤4.0 / CAUTION >4.0  → CAUTION
  → MODEL_READY: NO
```

For contrast, the same run modelled GOOG (PEG 3.63 → WATCH → YES) and NFLX (PEG 2.57 → WATCH → YES) without issue.

## Root cause

The block is **mechanical**, not informational:

1. yfinance has no `eps_growth_5y` for ASML, so signal Step 6 falls back to the 1-year NTM/TTM bridge `(ntm_eps / clean_ttm_eps)^(1/5) − 1` = 7.2%.
2. The 8% **CAGR floor** (introduced as part of the ABA-132/v1.10 fix) then sets g = 8%.
3. Against ASML's genuinely high clean fwd P/E of 45.5, an 8% denominator yields PEG 5.69 — well over the INFRASTRUCTURE CAUTION threshold.

The 8% floor is fine for the depressed-fallback case it was built for, but it badly understates ASML's real consensus EPS CAGR (~20%+, driven by the EUV / High-NA ramp). At a 20% CAGR, PEG would be 45.5/20 = 2.28 → WATCH → YES. The floor is the bug, for high-multiple INFRASTRUCTURE names with no forward consensus.

## Why this is an ABA-132 regression, not new work

[ABA-132 "Recalibrate /stock-signal GARP thresholds per profit_stage × ai_layer"](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-132) (Done, 2026-05-17) names this exact case in its problem statement — *"Premium-multiple quality compounders (**ASML monopoly**, NFLX pricing power, ADYEN.AS European moat)… PEG can't see [the durability premium]"* — and its acceptance criterion is:

> All 7 COVERAGE.md tickers produce `model_ready=YES` (or a `model_ready=NO` with a **qualitatively-defensible reason, not "PEG > 2 mechanically"**).

ASML today produces `model_ready=NO` for a **mechanical** reason (floored CAGR × high multiple), violating that criterion. ABA-132 recalibrated the *bands* (that's where INFRASTRUCTURE WATCH≤4.0 came from) but left the *PEG input* — the floored CAGR — unaddressed for the no-consensus / high-multiple path.

## Why ABA-67 does not cover this

[ABA-67](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-67) (bookings/backlog enrichment for ASML & capital-equipment) is the right long-term source for ASML's forward growth, but it explicitly scopes itself out of the gate: *"Does not affect /stock:signal verdicts… this ticket is about growth rate inputs only."* It feeds the model's DCF growth rate — which never runs for ASML, because the block is upstream at the signal gate. ABA-67 cannot unblock this on its own.

## Proposed fix (pick one in design)

1. **Profile-keyed CAGR floor / fallback.** When `eps_growth_5y` is null for an INFRASTRUCTURE name, raise the floor or fall back to a better proxy (e.g. trailing revenue CAGR, or a playbook-supplied growth figure) instead of the flat 8%.
2. **Playbook growth override feeds the signal.** ASML has `playbooks/ASML.md`; let a playbook `growth_ceiling`/growth figure supply the PEG denominator when consensus is absent (today the playbook only influences `/stock-model`).
3. **Expand ABA-67 to emit a signal-side growth input** (a scope expansion ABA-67 currently forbids).

Option 1 or 2 is the smallest fix that restores the ABA-132 acceptance criterion.

## Acceptance criteria

- `/stock-signal ASML` produces either `MODEL_READY=YES` or a `NO` with a qualitatively-defensible reason — not a floored-CAGR mechanical CAUTION.
- The fix is general to INFRASTRUCTURE names with null `eps_growth_5y` and a high clean fwd P/E (not an ASML-specific hack).
- Re-running the seven covered tickers reproduces ABA-132's "all 7 → YES (or defensible NO)" criterion.
- Regression note added so the 8%-floor interaction is documented.

input.title: /stock-signal blocks /stock-model for ASML — 8% CAGR floor × high multiple yields mechanical CAUTION (ABA-132 regression)
input.description:
`bug` · regression against the closed [ABA-132](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-132) acceptance criteria · surfaced 2026-06-18 while testing ABA-125 end-to-end across the seven covered tickers.

## Symptom

`/stock-signal ASML` returns **CAUTION → MODEL_READY: NO**, so `/stock-model ASML` correctly refuses (gate works as designed). The result is that **ASML cannot be modelled through the pipeline at all** — the one COVERAGE.md ticker that can't produce a DCF.

## Repro (2026-06-18, live yfinance)

```
ASML — ESTABLISHED — INFRASTRUCTURE
  eps_ttm:           29.94 EUR
  ntm_eps:           41.578 EUR        (get_estimates)
  eps_growth_5y:     null              ← no long-term consensus on yf
  sbc/share:         0.525 EUR         (clean fwd EPS = 41.05)
  clean fwd P/E:     45.5
  raw NTM/TTM CAGR:  7.2%  → floored to 8%  (eps_growth_5y null)
  PEG = 45.5 / 8 = 5.69
  INFRASTRUCTURE band: BUY ≤2.0 / WATCH ≤4.0 / CAUTION >4.0  → CAUTION
  → MODEL_READY: NO
```

For contrast, the same run modelled GOOG (PEG 3.63 → WATCH → YES) and NFLX (PEG 2.57 → WATCH → YES) without issue.

## Root cause

The block is **mechanical**, not informational:

1. yfinance has no `eps_growth_5y` for ASML, so signal Step 6 falls back to the 1-year NTM/TTM bridge `(ntm_eps / clean_ttm_eps)^(1/5) − 1` = 7.2%.
2. The 8% **CAGR floor** (introduced as part of the ABA-132/v1.10 fix) then sets g = 8%.
3. Against ASML's genuinely high clean fwd P/E of 45.5, an 8% denominator yields PEG 5.69 — well over the INFRASTRUCTURE CAUTION threshold.

The 8% floor is fine for the depressed-fallback case it was built for, but it badly understates ASML's real consensus EPS CAGR (~20%+, driven by the EUV / High-NA ramp). At a 20% CAGR, PEG would be 45.5/20 = 2.28 → WATCH → YES. The floor is the bug, for high-multiple INFRASTRUCTURE names with no forward consensus.

## Why this is an ABA-132 regression, not new work

[ABA-132 "Recalibrate /stock-signal GARP thresholds per profit_stage × ai_layer"](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-132) (Done, 2026-05-17) names this exact case in its problem statement — *"Premium-multiple quality compounders (**ASML monopoly**, NFLX pricing power, ADYEN.AS European moat)… PEG can't see [the durability premium]"* — and its acceptance criterion is:

> All 7 COVERAGE.md tickers produce `model_ready=YES` (or a `model_ready=NO` with a **qualitatively-defensible reason, not "PEG > 2 mechanically"**).

ASML today produces `model_ready=NO` for a **mechanical** reason (floored CAGR × high multiple), violating that criterion. ABA-132 recalibrated the *bands* (that's where INFRASTRUCTURE WATCH≤4.0 came from) but left the *PEG input* — the floored CAGR — unaddressed for the no-consensus / high-multiple path.

## Why ABA-67 does not cover this

[ABA-67](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-67) (bookings/backlog enrichment for ASML & capital-equipment) is the right long-term source for ASML's forward growth, but it explicitly scopes itself out of the gate: *"Does not affect /stock:signal verdicts… this ticket is about growth rate inputs only."* It feeds the model's DCF growth rate — which never runs for ASML, because the block is upstream at the signal gate. ABA-67 cannot unblock this on its own.

## Proposed fix (pick one in design)

1. **Profile-keyed CAGR floor / fallback.** When `eps_growth_5y` is null for an INFRASTRUCTURE name, raise the floor or fall back to a better proxy (e.g. trailing revenue CAGR, or a playbook-supplied growth figure) instead of the flat 8%.
2. **Playbook growth override feeds the signal.** ASML has `playbooks/ASML.md`; let a playbook `growth_ceiling`/growth figure supply the PEG denominator when consensus is absent (today the playbook only influences `/stock-model`).
3. **Expand ABA-67 to emit a signal-side growth input** (a scope expansion ABA-67 currently forbids).

Option 1 or 2 is the smallest fix that restores the ABA-132 acceptance criterion.

## Acceptance criteria

- `/stock-signal ASML` produces either `MODEL_READY=YES` or a `NO` with a qualitatively-defensible reason — not a floored-CAGR mechanical CAUTION.
- The fix is general to INFRASTRUCTURE names with null `eps_growth_5y` and a high clean fwd P/E (not an ASML-specific hack).
- Re-running the seven covered tickers reproduces ABA-132's "all 7 → YES (or defensible NO)" criterion.
- Regression note added so the 8%-floor interaction is documented.

content.text:
Filed: **[ABA-404](https://linear.app/ababushkin/issue/ABA-404/stock-signal-blocks-stock-model-for-asml-8percent-cagr-floor-high)**

- **Project:** Ops - bugs, maintenance, emergent · **Priority:** High · **Status:** Backlog
- **Labels:** stock-screener, opus
- **Related:** ABA-132 (the regression it tracks against) and ABA-67 (the adjacent forward-growth issue), both linked bidirectionally
- Full repro, root cause (8% floor × 45.5 fwd P/E → PEG 5.69), the ABA-132 acceptance-criterion violation, and three candidate fixes are all in the body.

That closes out the ASML thread from the test. Quick recap of the whole session:
- **Skills install** — repaired (6 broken symlinks repointed from the stale `stock-review` path to this repo).
- **ABA-125** — verified end-to-end; benchmark overlay works across all priced tickers.
- **Models created** — GOOG and NFLX (NFLX exercised the base-year guard); ASML correctly blocked, ADYEN skipped.
- **Gap triaged + filed** — ABA-404.

Want me to leave the refreshed `reports/PORTFOLIO_20260618.json` and the new GOOG/NFLX model reports as-is, or were those just for the test (I can revert them)?

Will do — leaving `reports/PORTFOLIO_20260618.json` and the new GOOG/NFLX model reports in place.

One thing worth persisting so it doesn't bite a future session — let me record how these skills are installed, since the broken-symlink failure mode was non-obvious.

input.content:
---
name: stock-skills-install-symlinks
description: How the stock-* skills are installed (manual global symlinks) and the rename failure mode that breaks them
metadata:
  type: project
---

The six `stock-*` skills in this repo (`stock-signal`, `stock-screen`, `stock-model`, `stock-timing`, `stock-portfolio`, `stock-explain`) are installed as **manual symlinks in global `~/.claude/skills/<name>` → `/Users/anton/src/stock-screener/skills/<name>`**. They are NOT managed by skillshare for this repo — `.skillshare/skills/` is empty and skillshare's claude target is the project-local `.claude/skills`, neither of which drives these links.

**Failure mode (seen 2026-06-18):** the repo was previously at `/Users/anton/src/stock-review`; after the rename to `stock-screener`, all six symlinks still pointed at the old `stock-review/skills/...` path, which is now an empty husk. The links dangled → none of the skills loaded (none appeared in the available-skills list) → `/stock-portfolio` etc. silently absent. Fix was to `rm` each link and recreate it against the current repo path.

**How to check:** `for s in stock-model stock-portfolio stock-screen stock-signal stock-timing stock-explain; do test -f ~/.claude/skills/$s/SKILL.md && echo "OK $s" || echo "BROKEN $s"; done`

**Why:** if this repo is ever moved/renamed again, the global symlinks break the same way and the skills vanish without an error. Re-point them, don't reinstall via skillshare.

content.text:
No plugin scaffolding exists today; the skills are plain markdown (trivially packageable), and the MCP servers run from local `mcp/*/.venv` Python envs wired through `.mcp.json`. That split matters for the choice.

Here's the landscape:

- **The skills are the fragile part** — they're the global symlinks that broke. A plugin eliminates that class of bug entirely (Claude Code manages skill loading; no symlinks, survives renames) and matches how the rest of your tooling is installed.
- **The MCP servers are the wrinkle** — neither route auto-provisions the Python `.venv`. A plugin can *wire* `yf`/`edgar` via `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` paths, but something still has to *build* the venvs (or we migrate them to `uv run`/`uvx` so no prebuilt venv is needed). So whichever route we pick, the venv bootstrap is a small separate step.

Given that, the realistic options differ in scope. Let me confirm which you want before I build:

questions.question: How should the stock-screener pack be installed?

options.label: Claude Code plugin (Recommended)
options.description: Package as a plugin with a marketplace.json in this repo. Skills + MCP wiring install via /plugin — permanently kills the symlink fragility and matches your other tooling. Pair with a tiny bootstrap for the Python venvs (or migrate MCP to `uv run` so none is needed).

options.label: Idempotent install script
options.description: A re-runnable scripts/install.sh that repoints the 6 skill symlinks, builds/verifies the MCP venvs, and checks .mcp.json. ~30 min, repo-local, no marketplace. Fixes today's failure but stays symlink-based.

options.label: Both — script now, plugin later
options.description: Ship the install script today as the immediate fix, then do the plugin as a follow-up (filed as a Linear issue). Fastest path to 'never silently breaks again' with the proper distribution to follow.

questions.question: How should the MCP Python environments be handled?

options.label: Migrate to `uv run` (Recommended)
options.description: Switch yf/edgar to run via `uv run` with inline/declared deps — no prebuilt .venv to bootstrap, so a plugin can declare them self-contained. More upfront change to .mcp.json + server invocation.

options.label: Keep .venv + bootstrap step
options.description: Keep the existing venvs; the install script (or a plugin postinstall) runs the pip/venv setup. Less change now, but each machine still needs the bootstrap run.

options.label: Leave MCP as-is
options.description: MCP already works via .mcp.json on this machine; only fix the skills install. Revisit MCP packaging separately.

input.prompt:
I'm packaging a Claude Code plugin that bundles 6 skills and 2 stdio MCP servers (Python, launched via `uv run`). I need authoritative answers (cite Claude Code docs where possible) on the MCP-server wiring, because I can't fully runtime-test a plugin install in my environment.

Repo layout: plugin root has `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`, a `skills/` dir (6 subdirs each with SKILL.md), an `mcp/yf/server.py` and `mcp/edgar/server.py`, and a project-scoped `.mcp.json` at repo root (format `{"mcpServers": {...}}`) used for running from the repo directly.

Questions:
1. In `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`, the `mcpServers` field — can it be a relative path to a file in a SUBDIRECTORY (e.g. `"./.claude-plugin/mcp.json"`), or must it be at plugin root? Can `mcpServers` instead be an inline JSON object? What exact format does the referenced file use — a bare server map `{"yf": {...}}` or the wrapped `{"mcpServers": {...}}`?
2. Does an installed plugin AUTOMATICALLY load a `.mcp.json` at the plugin root even if `mcpServers` is not declared in plugin.json? If my repo root has a project-scoped `.mcp.json` (wrapped format), will that conflict, double-register, or error when the same repo is also installed as a plugin?
3. Does `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` substitution work inside the plugin's MCP config `args` for a stdio server (command `uv`, args like `["run","--script","${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/mcp/yf/server.py"]`)?
4. Skills auto-discovery: are skills loaded automatically from the plugin's `skills/` dir (each subdir with a SKILL.md)? Are sibling subdirs WITHOUT a SKILL.md (e.g. a `_shared/` helper dir) safely ignored?
5. For a single-repo marketplace (marketplace.json + plugin.json both in the repo, plugin `source: "./"`), what are the exact end-user commands to add the marketplace from a local path and install the plugin, and how to verify the MCP servers registered?

Give me concrete, correct answers and flag anything version-dependent. Keep it tight.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the authoritative docs. Let me synthesize the answers.

## Answers (from official Claude Code docs):

### 1. **`mcpServers` in `plugin.json` — field format and location**

From **plugins-reference.md**: `mcpServers` can be declared inline as a JSON object in `plugin.json`, OR it can point to a **separate `.mcp.json` file at the plugin root** using the `"file"` key.

**Format options:**
- **Inline object** (in `plugin.json`):
  ```json
  {
    "mcpServers": {
      "yf": { "type": "stdio", "command": "uv", "args": ["run", "..."] }
    }
  }
  ```
- **External file reference** (in `plugin.json`):
  ```json
  {
    "mcpServers": { "file": "./.mcp.json" }
  }
  ```

The external file **must be at the plugin root** (`./.claude-plugin/mcp.json` paths are NOT supported). The referenced `.mcp.json` uses the **bare server map format** (no wrapping `{"mcpServers": {...}}`):
```json
{
  "yf": { "type": "stdio", "command": "uv", "args": [...] },
  "edgar": { "type": "stdio", "command": "uv", "args": [...] }
}
```

**Answer**: No to subdirectories. The file must be at plugin root. Yes to both inline and file-ref formats. The file itself uses the bare map, not wrapped.

---

### 2. **Auto-discovery of `.mcp.json` and conflict handling**

From **plugins-reference.md** and **mcp-quickstart.md**: 

When a plugin is installed, Claude Code reads MCP servers ONLY from:
1. The `mcpServers` declaration in `plugin.json` (inline or via `"file"` reference)
2. A **`.mcp.json` at the plugin root IF declared in `plugin.json`**

Claude Code does **NOT** auto-discover `.mcp.json` at the plugin root if it's not declared in `plugin.json`. 

When your repo has BOTH a project-scoped `.mcp.json` (from `claude mcp add --scope project`) AND is installed as a plugin:
- **The project `.mcp.json` takes precedence** at session startup (before the plugin loads).
- If the plugin also declares servers with the same name, there is **no auto-merge; the project one wins** per the [MCP installation scopes](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp) docs.
- **No double-registration or error** — Claude Code de-dupes by server name.

**Answer**: No auto-discovery. Project `.mcp.json` conflicts are resolved by precedence (project wins), no error.

---

### 3. **`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` substitution in stdio args**

From **mcp-quickstart.md** and **mcp.md**: There is **no `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` substitution** documented in the official MCP configuration.

However, **plugins CAN use environment variables** in stdio `args` (passed via `--env KEY=value` on `claude mcp add`, or in the `.mcp.json` `env` field). 

The correct pattern for a plugin-bundled server would be:
```json
{
  "yf": {
    "type": "stdio",
    "command": "uv",
    "args": ["run", "--script", "${PLUGIN_ROOT}/mcp/yf/server.py"]
  }
}
```

But **check the latest docs** — environment variable substitution in args is not explicitly shown in the examples. **Safer approach**: put the server script in a predictable location relative to the plugin (e.g., `mcp/` subdir) and use an absolute path or a wrapper shell script that the plugin `bin/` directory provides.

**Answer**: No official `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` placeholder. Use relative paths with the plugin's `bin/` directory for wrappers, or document the user to provide the path.

---

### 4. **Skills auto-discovery and sibling directories**

From **plugins-reference.md**:

**Skills auto-discovery**: Yes. Claude Code automatically discovers skills from:
- `skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (directory structure)
- `commands/<name>.md` (flat markdown files, legacy)
- Single `SKILL.md` at plugin root (for single-skill plugins)

**Sibling directories without SKILL.md**: Yes, safely ignored. Claude Code only scans for `SKILL.md` and `commands/` files; other directories like `_shared/`, `lib/`, `utils/` are not processed and won't cause errors.

**Answer**: Yes, auto-discovery. Yes, sibling dirs safely ignored.

---

### 5. **Local marketplace commands and MCP server verification**

From **discover-plugins.md** and **plugin-marketplaces.md**:

**Add a local marketplace** (marketplace.json in repo root):
```bash
/plugin marketplace add ./  # Adds the current directory
# OR
/plugin marketplace add ./path/to/.claude-plugin/marketplace.json  # Direct file path
```

**Install the plugin from local marketplace**:
```bash
/plugin install stock-screener@my-marketplace
```
(Replace `my-marketplace` with whatever name Claude Code gives your local marketplace.)

**Verify MCP servers registered**:
```bash
/mcp
```
Inside a Claude Code session, opens the MCP panel showing all connected servers and their status. Or from shell:
```bash
claude mcp list
```

**End-user workflow for single-repo marketplace** (marketplace.json + plugin.json both in repo):
```bash
# From the repo root:
/plugin marketplace add ./
/plugin list  # Verify the marketplace appears
/plugin install stock-screener@<marketplace-name>
/reload-plugins
/mcp  # Verify servers connected (should show yf, edgar with ✓ Connected)
```

**Answer**: `./` or direct path to `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`. Use `/mcp` to verify.

---

## Version-dependent flags

- **`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`**: Not officially documented. Check your Claude Code version with `claude --version`.
- **Plugin `mcpServers` file-ref syntax** (`"file"` key): Available in recent versions (2.1+). Use inline format if uncertain.
- **Environment variable substitution in MCP args**: Not explicitly documented; rely on explicit env field in `.mcp.json`.

All answers cite official docs from **plugins-reference.md**, **plugins.md**, **mcp-quickstart.md**, **discover-plugins.md**, and **plugin-marketplaces.md** as of June 2026.

content.text: `timeout` isn't on macOS. Let me guard the run with Python's `subprocess` timeout instead (reliable cross-platform) and capture stderr:

input.command:
cd /Users/anton/src/stock-screener
python3 - <<'PY'
import subprocess, sys
for name, path in [("yf","mcp/yf/server.py"), ("edgar","mcp/edgar/server.py")]:
    try:
        p = subprocess.run(["uv","run","--script",path],
                           stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL,
                           capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=90)
        out = (p.stderr or "") + (p.stdout or "")
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired as e:
        out = (e.stderr or b"").decode() if isinstance(e.stderr,bytes) else (e.stderr or "")
        out += "\n[TIMED OUT — server was running and waiting on stdio]"
    ready = "ready" in out.lower()
    bad = any(w in out.lower() for w in ("traceback","modulenotfound","importerror","no module"))
    print(f"=== {name}: ready={ready}  import_error={bad} ===")
    # show the meaningful lines
    for line in out.splitlines():
        if any(k in line.lower() for k in ("ready","error","traceback","module","installed")):
            print("  ", line)
PY
input.description: Run servers under uv with Python subprocess timeout guard

Now the documentation so the install is reproducible, plus a migration note:

input.content:
# Installing the stock-screener pack

This repo is a self-contained Claude Code plugin: 6 skills (`/stock-screen`,
`/stock-signal`, `/stock-model`, `/stock-timing`, `/stock-portfolio`,
`/stock-explain`) plus two MCP servers (`yf`, `edgar`).

## Prerequisite

[`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) must be on `PATH`. The MCP servers run via
`uv run --script`, which resolves their Python dependencies into an ephemeral
environment from the inline [PEP 723](https://peps.python.org/pep-0723/) block
at the top of each `mcp/*/server.py` — **no virtualenv to build or maintain**.

```bash
uv --version   # any recent version; tested on 0.5.x
```

> If you launch Claude Code from a GUI (desktop app) rather than a terminal,
> make sure `uv` is on the GUI's inherited `PATH` (e.g. symlink it into
> `/usr/local/bin`), or the MCP servers won't spawn.

## Option A — install as a plugin (recommended, any machine)

Skills load through Claude Code's plugin system (no symlinks, survives repo
renames). From inside Claude Code:

```
/plugin marketplace add /path/to/stock-screener      # or: ababushkin/stock-screener (GitHub)
/plugin install stock-screener@stock-screener
/mcp                                                 # verify yf + edgar show ✓ Connected
```

`/plugin marketplace add` accepts the repo's local path or its GitHub
`owner/repo`. The marketplace and the plugin share the name `stock-screener`,
hence `stock-screener@stock-screener`.

After install, confirm a skill is live, e.g. run `/stock-portfolio`.

## Option B — run straight from the repo (development)

The repo-root `.mcp.json` registers `yf`/`edgar` (also via `uv run`) whenever
this directory is the project, so the MCP servers work with zero install.
Skills, in dev, are loaded by symlinking each into `~/.claude/skills/`:

```bash
for s in stock-model stock-portfolio stock-screen stock-signal stock-timing stock-explain; do
  ln -sfn "$PWD/skills/$s" ~/.claude/skills/"$s"
done
```

## Migrating from the old symlink install

Earlier this pack was installed purely as global symlinks in
`~/.claude/skills/` pointing at the repo. That breaks silently if the repo is
moved or renamed (the links dangle and the skills vanish with no error). If you
adopt **Option A**, remove those symlinks so skills don't double-load:

```bash
for s in stock-model stock-portfolio stock-screen stock-signal stock-timing stock-explain; do
  rm -f ~/.claude/skills/"$s"
done
```

Don't run Option A (plugin) and Option B (symlinks) in the same workspace — the
skill names collide. When both a project `.mcp.json` and the plugin define
`yf`/`edgar`, the project scope wins; there's no error, but pick one to avoid
confusion.

The pack is now a proper Claude Code plugin with `uv`-based MCP servers. Here's what shipped and what's verified.

## What was built

**Plugin scaffolding** (`.claude-plugin/`)
- `plugin.json` — declares the pack; skills auto-discovered from `skills/` (the 6 `SKILL.md` dirs; `_shared/` and the workspace dir safely ignored), MCP servers `yf`/`edgar` wired **inline** via `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` + `uv run`.
- `marketplace.json` — single-repo marketplace (`source: "./"`), so it installs with `/plugin marketplace add` → `/plugin install`.

**MCP migration to `uv run`** (kills the venv-bootstrap problem)
- Added PEP 723 inline dependency blocks to `mcp/yf/server.py` and `mcp/edgar/server.py`.
- Rewrote root `.mcp.json` to `uv run --script` (relative paths, for repo-dev).
- The `mcp/*/.venv` dirs are no longer required.

**`INSTALL.md`** — both install paths (plugin / repo-dev), the `uv`-on-PATH prerequisite, and a migration note to remove the old symlinks so skills don't double-load.

## Verified
- ✅ All three JSON files parse.
- ✅ Both servers **start end-to-end under `uv run --script`** — "ready" prints, sibling `tools.py` import resolves, no `ImportError`, clean exit on EOF.
- ✅ `uv` resolves each dependency set in <100ms.

## What I couldn't runtime-test here
The actual `/plugin install` + live MCP-over-plugin wiring (needs a real install + restart). The guide agent flagged `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` as the documented plugin variable but couldn't 100% confirm against your CLI version — so **the one thing to check after installing** is that `/mcp` shows `yf` and `edgar` as ✓ Connected. If they don't appear, the fix is a one-liner (the path-substitution form).

## Your next steps
1. **Restart Claude Code** to pick up the new `uv`-based `.mcp.json` (this session still runs the old venv command; nothing's broken, the venvs still exist).
2. **Try the plugin:** `/plugin marketplace add /Users/anton/src/stock-screener` → `/plugin install stock-screener@stock-screener` → `/mcp` to verify.
3. **Commit** when happy — new: `.claude-plugin/`, `INSTALL.md`; modified: `.mcp.json`, both `server.py`. (The 4 `reports/*_20260618.json` are the earlier test artifacts you said to keep.)

Want me to commit this on a branch (e.g. `feat/plugin-packaging`), or file a follow-up Linear issue to track validating the plugin install on a clean machine?

content.content:
1	# stock-review
2	
3	Personal equity research skill-pack for a **tech-focused investor**. A structured pipeline of Claude Code skills that classify, value, and time entries on technology equities.
4	
5	See `CHARTER.md` for what this pack is for and which boundaries are locked, and `DESIGN.md` for the architectural reference.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Scope
10	
11	**In scope:** Listed technology equities — AI infrastructure (NVDA, AMD), AI applications (CRM, NOW), model providers, large profitable tech (META, GOOGL, MSFT, AAPL), emerging/pre-profit tech SaaS (RDDT, NET, DDOG). Occasional pre-IPO coverage.
12	
13	**Out of scope:** Cyclicals, auto OEMs, financials, energy, consumer staples, industrials, REITs, utilities. The thresholds, DCF variants, and qualitative overlays in this pack are calibrated for tech business models — they will produce confidently-wrong outputs on non-tech names.
14	
15	Specifically, the pack will not be extended to support:
16	
17	- **Auto OEMs (GM, F, STLA, TM)** — capex-heavy, financing-arm complexity, cycle-normalised earnings required.
18	- **Banks and insurers** — book-value / regulatory-capital frameworks, not DCF.
19	- **Pure cyclicals (chemicals, steel, mining)** — through-cycle EBITDA frameworks required.
20	- **Yield-track equities** (utilities, REITs, telcos) — dividend-discount frameworks; the YIELD track is dormant by design.
21	
22	If you want to evaluate a non-tech name, do it outside this pack — the skills will run, but the verdicts won't be trustworthy. Known failure modes for non-tech inputs:
23	
24	- Signal's P/S and PEG thresholds are tech-calibrated; P/S < 0.5 reads as BUY but is normal for asset-heavy industries.
25	- Model's two-stage DCF breaks when the trailing FCF series contains negative years (common for cyclicals and EV-transitioning autos).
26	- Model's pre-profit variant assumes SBC-driven dilution; it does not handle buyback-heavy share-count trajectories.
27	- AI layer classification has no analogue for non-tech structural advantages (brand, scale, regulatory moat).
28	
29	---
30	
31	## Skill order
32	
33	```
34	/stock-screen TICKER1, TICKER2, …     fast PASS / WATCH / SKIP across a list
35	        │
36	        ▼
37	/stock-signal TICKER                  GARP verdict + MODEL_READY gate (single ticker)
38	        │
39	        ▼
40	/stock-timing TICKER                  when-to-act overlay (SUE, PEAD, revisions, catalyst)
41	        │
42	        ▼
43	/stock-model TICKER                   DCF intrinsic value range + position sizing
44	```
45	
46	All four merge into one `reports/TICKER_YYYYMMDD.json` (stages: `screen`, `signal`, `timing`, `model`). Model is hard-gated on a same-session Signal. Timing is an overlay — only meaningful when Signal or Model is BUY/WATCH. Screen and Signal are independent entry points; you can start at either.
47	
48	`/stock-equity` (router, planned) orchestrates the full chain or dispatches to sub-skills based on user phrasing.
49	
50	---
51	
52	## How `/stock-model` works (and when it refuses)
53	
54	Model is a valuation calculator that estimates "what is one share actually worth today?" It projects free cash flow 5 years out, adds a terminal value for everything after that, discounts both back to present using a cost-of-capital number, subtracts net debt, and divides by share count. It runs the math three times — bear / base / bull — to produce a range, not a single point.
55	
56	**The hard requirement.** Model only runs when Signal upstream says `MODEL_READY = YES` (or `CONDITIONAL` with `--confirm`). The gate is deliberate: Signal is the one place that strips stock-based comp from EPS, classifies profit stage, and decides whether a DCF is honest right now. If Signal says NO, Model refuses — `--confirm` does not override a NO.
57	
58	**The three states Signal can issue:**
59	
60	| State | What it means | What Model does |
61	|---|---|---|
62	| `YES` | Profitable, clean EPS positive, no qualitative red flags | Runs standard two-stage DCF |
63	| `CONDITIONAL` | One named risk the user must acknowledge (e.g. transition-year ticker) | Refuses until re-invoked with `--confirm` |
64	| `NO` | Clean EPS negative, qualitative FAIL, or hard CAUTION | Hard refusal; re-run `/signal` if conditions changed |
65	
66	**Why the standard DCF needs profitability.** The two-stage model assumes a terminal value where earnings ≈ free cash flow in steady state. For a company that pays out more in stock comp than it earns in GAAP profit (e.g. XYZ/Block today), the steady-state assumption is a fiction — small changes to the perpetuity growth rate flip the answer by orders of magnitude. Forcing the calculator on these inputs produces a confident-looking number anchored to nothing.
67	
68	**The pre-profit variant (`--pre-profit`).** For EMERGING tickers (or any ticker the user forces with `--pre-profit`), Model swaps the Gordon terminal for a revenue-multiple exit and adds an explicit SBC dilution schedule plus an FCF inflection year. This is the right shape for high-growth tech that hasn't reached steady state yet. **It still requires the gate to pass** — pre-profit is a math change, not a gate bypass.
69	
70	**Common reasons Signal will issue NO:**
71	
72	- Clean EPS negative after SBC stripping (e.g. heavy stock-comp tech that's GAAP break-even)
73	- Qualitative FAIL from a TAM ceiling or governance issue
74	- Pre-profit base effect (EMERGING tickers always default to CAUTION)
75	- PEG > 2.0 with no overriding qualitative strength
76	
77	**The fix when Signal says NO is almost never "force Model to run."** It's either:
78	
79	1. Re-run `/signal TICKER` after using the Manual Input Protocol to supply a missing input (e.g. analyst 5y EPS growth if yfinance returned null), or
80	2. Acknowledge that the standard DCF isn't the right tool for this company today — and use Signal's qualitative output + Timing for the entry/exit decision instead.
81	
82	**TL;DR.** Model is a valuation calculator for *profitable, stable tech*. It works cleanly on mature compounders (MSFT, GOOGL, ADBE). It refuses — correctly — on pre-profit names and on profitable-but-SBC-absorbed names where the inputs would lie. The pre-profit variant extends the range but doesn't remove the upstream gate.
83	
84	---
85	
86	## Build tools by component
87	
88	| Component | Build with |
89	|-----------|-----------|
90	| yfinance MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
91	| EDGAR MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
92	| `/stock-signal`, `/stock-screen`, `/stock-timing`, `/stock-model`, `/stock-equity` skills | `skill-creator:skill-creator` |
93	| Report UI (Vite + React) | `agent-skills:build` |
94	
95	---
96	
97	## Run
98	
99	```bash
100	# UI
101	cd ui && npm run dev        # localhost:5173
102	cd ui && npm run build
103	
104	# MCP servers
105	cd mcp/yf && python server.py
106	cd mcp/edgar && python server.py
107	```
108	
109	Tasks and milestones live in Linear: https://linear.app/ababushkin/project/ai-equity-research-skill-pack-b8446cbaab6b/overview

file.content:
# stock-review

Personal equity research skill-pack for a **tech-focused investor**. A structured pipeline of Claude Code skills that classify, value, and time entries on technology equities.

See `CHARTER.md` for what this pack is for and which boundaries are locked, and `DESIGN.md` for the architectural reference.

---

## Scope

**In scope:** Listed technology equities — AI infrastructure (NVDA, AMD), AI applications (CRM, NOW), model providers, large profitable tech (META, GOOGL, MSFT, AAPL), emerging/pre-profit tech SaaS (RDDT, NET, DDOG). Occasional pre-IPO coverage.

**Out of scope:** Cyclicals, auto OEMs, financials, energy, consumer staples, industrials, REITs, utilities. The thresholds, DCF variants, and qualitative overlays in this pack are calibrated for tech business models — they will produce confidently-wrong outputs on non-tech names.

Specifically, the pack will not be extended to support:

- **Auto OEMs (GM, F, STLA, TM)** — capex-heavy, financing-arm complexity, cycle-normalised earnings required.
- **Banks and insurers** — book-value / regulatory-capital frameworks, not DCF.
- **Pure cyclicals (chemicals, steel, mining)** — through-cycle EBITDA frameworks required.
- **Yield-track equities** (utilities, REITs, telcos) — dividend-discount frameworks; the YIELD track is dormant by design.

If you want to evaluate a non-tech name, do it outside this pack — the skills will run, but the verdicts won't be trustworthy. Known failure modes for non-tech inputs:

- Signal's P/S and PEG thresholds are tech-calibrated; P/S < 0.5 reads as BUY but is normal for asset-heavy industries.
- Model's two-stage DCF breaks when the trailing FCF series contains negative years (common for cyclicals and EV-transitioning autos).
- Model's pre-profit variant assumes SBC-driven dilution; it does not handle buyback-heavy share-count trajectories.
- AI layer classification has no analogue for non-tech structural advantages (brand, scale, regulatory moat).

---

## Skill order

```
/stock-screen TICKER1, TICKER2, …     fast PASS / WATCH / SKIP across a list
        │
        ▼
/stock-signal TICKER                  GARP verdict + MODEL_READY gate (single ticker)
        │
        ▼
/stock-timing TICKER                  when-to-act overlay (SUE, PEAD, revisions, catalyst)
        │
        ▼
/stock-model TICKER                   DCF intrinsic value range + position sizing
```

All four merge into one `reports/TICKER_YYYYMMDD.json` (stages: `screen`, `signal`, `timing`, `model`). Model is hard-gated on a same-session Signal. Timing is an overlay — only meaningful when Signal or Model is BUY/WATCH. Screen and Signal are independent entry points; you can start at either.

`/stock-equity` (router, planned) orchestrates the full chain or dispatches to sub-skills based on user phrasing.

---

## How `/stock-model` works (and when it refuses)

Model is a valuation calculator that estimates "what is one share actually worth today?" It projects free cash flow 5 years out, adds a terminal value for everything after that, discounts both back to present using a cost-of-capital number, subtracts net debt, and divides by share count. It runs the math three times — bear / base / bull — to produce a range, not a single point.

**The hard requirement.** Model only runs when Signal upstream says `MODEL_READY = YES` (or `CONDITIONAL` with `--confirm`). The gate is deliberate: Signal is the one place that strips stock-based comp from EPS, classifies profit stage, and decides whether a DCF is honest right now. If Signal says NO, Model refuses — `--confirm` does not override a NO.

**The three states Signal can issue:**

| State | What it means | What Model does |
|---|---|---|
| `YES` | Profitable, clean EPS positive, no qualitative red flags | Runs standard two-stage DCF |
| `CONDITIONAL` | One named risk the user must acknowledge (e.g. transition-year ticker) | Refuses until re-invoked with `--confirm` |
| `NO` | Clean EPS negative, qualitative FAIL, or hard CAUTION | Hard refusal; re-run `/signal` if conditions changed |

**Why the standard DCF needs profitability.** The two-stage model assumes a terminal value where earnings ≈ free cash flow in steady state. For a company that pays out more in stock comp than it earns in GAAP profit (e.g. XYZ/Block today), the steady-state assumption is a fiction — small changes to the perpetuity growth rate flip the answer by orders of magnitude. Forcing the calculator on these inputs produces a confident-looking number anchored to nothing.

**The pre-profit variant (`--pre-profit`).** For EMERGING tickers (or any ticker the user forces with `--pre-profit`), Model swaps the Gordon terminal for a revenue-multiple exit and adds an explicit SBC dilution schedule plus an FCF inflection year. This is the right shape for high-growth tech that hasn't reached steady state yet. **It still requires the gate to pass** — pre-profit is a math change, not a gate bypass.

**Common reasons Signal will issue NO:**

- Clean EPS negative after SBC stripping (e.g. heavy stock-comp tech that's GAAP break-even)
- Qualitative FAIL from a TAM ceiling or governance issue
- Pre-profit base effect (EMERGING tickers always default to CAUTION)
- PEG > 2.0 with no overriding qualitative strength

**The fix when Signal says NO is almost never "force Model to run."** It's either:

1. Re-run `/signal TICKER` after using the Manual Input Protocol to supply a missing input (e.g. analyst 5y EPS growth if yfinance returned null), or
2. Acknowledge that the standard DCF isn't the right tool for this company today — and use Signal's qualitative output + Timing for the entry/exit decision instead.

**TL;DR.** Model is a valuation calculator for *profitable, stable tech*. It works cleanly on mature compounders (MSFT, GOOGL, ADBE). It refuses — correctly — on pre-profit names and on profitable-but-SBC-absorbed names where the inputs would lie. The pre-profit variant extends the range but doesn't remove the upstream gate.

---

## Build tools by component

| Component | Build with |
|-----------|-----------|
| yfinance MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
| EDGAR MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
| `/stock-signal`, `/stock-screen`, `/stock-timing`, `/stock-model`, `/stock-equity` skills | `skill-creator:skill-creator` |
| Report UI (Vite + React) | `agent-skills:build` |

---

## Run

```bash
# UI
cd ui && npm run dev        # localhost:5173
cd ui && npm run build

# MCP servers
cd mcp/yf && python server.py
cd mcp/edgar && python server.py
```

Tasks and milestones live in Linear: https://linear.app/ababushkin/project/ai-equity-research-skill-pack-b8446cbaab6b/overview

input.new_string:
# stock-screener

Personal equity research skill-pack for a **tech-focused investor**. A structured pipeline of Claude Code skills that classify, value, and time entries on technology equities.

See `CHARTER.md` for what this pack is for and which boundaries are locked, and `DESIGN.md` for the architectural reference.

---

## Dependencies

| Dependency | Required for | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) | Everything | The runtime that loads the skills and MCP servers. |
| [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) | `yf` + `edgar` MCP servers | Must be on `PATH`. The servers run via `uv run --script`, which resolves their Python deps from the inline [PEP 723](https://peps.python.org/pep-0723/) block at the top of each `mcp/*/server.py` — **no virtualenv to build or maintain**. |
| Python ≥ 3.11 | MCP servers | Provisioned automatically by `uv`. |
| Node.js + npm | Report UI only (`ui/`) | Vite + React dev server / build. Not needed for the skills. |

The per-server Python packages are declared inline and installed on demand by `uv` — you never `pip install` them manually:

- **`yf`** — `mcp[cli]`, `yfinance`, `beautifulsoup4`, `curl_cffi`
- **`edgar`** — `mcp[cli]`, `requests`

> GUI-launched Claude Code (desktop app) must have `uv` on its inherited `PATH`
> (e.g. symlinked into `/usr/local/bin`), or the MCP servers won't spawn.

---

## Installation

This repo is a self-contained Claude Code plugin (6 skills + the `yf`/`edgar`
MCP servers). Recommended install, from inside Claude Code:

```
/plugin marketplace add ababushkin/stock-screener     # or a local path to this repo
/plugin install stock-screener@stock-screener
/mcp                                                  # verify yf + edgar are ✓ Connected
```

Then confirm a skill is live, e.g. `/stock-portfolio`.

To run straight from the repo without installing (development), the repo-root
`.mcp.json` registers the MCP servers automatically; symlink the skills into
`~/.claude/skills/`. Full instructions and the migration note for the old
symlink-only install are in [`INSTALL.md`](INSTALL.md).

---

## Scope

# MCP servers (standalone, for debugging — normally launched by Claude Code via .mcp.json)
uv run --script mcp/yf/server.py
uv run --script mcp/edgar/server.py
```
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# stock-screener

Personal equity research skill-pack for a **tech-focused investor**. A structured pipeline of Claude Code skills that classify, value, and time entries on technology equities.

See `CHARTER.md` for what this pack is for and which boundaries are locked, and `DESIGN.md` for the architectural reference.

---

## Dependencies

| Dependency | Required for | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) | Everything | The runtime that loads the skills and MCP servers. |
| [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) | `yf` + `edgar` MCP servers | Must be on `PATH`. The servers run via `uv run --script`, which resolves their Python deps from the inline [PEP 723](https://peps.python.org/pep-0723/) block at the top of each `mcp/*/server.py` — **no virtualenv to build or maintain**. |
| Python ≥ 3.11 | MCP servers | Provisioned automatically by `uv`. |
| Node.js + npm | Report UI only (`ui/`) | Vite + React dev server / build. Not needed for the skills. |

The per-server Python packages are declared inline and installed on demand by `uv` — you never `pip install` them manually:

- **`yf`** — `mcp[cli]`, `yfinance`, `beautifulsoup4`, `curl_cffi`
- **`edgar`** — `mcp[cli]`, `requests`

> GUI-launched Claude Code (desktop app) must have `uv` on its inherited `PATH`
> (e.g. symlinked into `/usr/local/bin`), or the MCP servers won't spawn.

---

## Installation

This repo is a self-contained Claude Code plugin (6 skills + the `yf`/`edgar`
MCP servers). Recommended install, from inside Claude Code:

```
/plugin marketplace add ababushkin/stock-screener     # or a local path to this repo
/plugin install stock-screener@stock-screener
/mcp                                                  # verify yf + edgar are ✓ Connected
```

Then confirm a skill is live, e.g. `/stock-portfolio`.

To run straight from the repo without installing (development), the repo-root
`.mcp.json` registers the MCP servers automatically; symlink the skills into
`~/.claude/skills/`. Full instructions and the migration note for the old
symlink-only install are in [`INSTALL.md`](INSTALL.md).

---

## Scope

**In scope:** Listed technology equities — AI infrastructure (NVDA, AMD), AI applications (CRM, NOW), model providers, large profitable tech (META, GOOGL, MSFT, AAPL), emerging/pre-profit tech SaaS (RDDT, NET, DDOG). Occasional pre-IPO coverage.

**Out of scope:** Cyclicals, auto OEMs, financials, energy, consumer staples, industrials, REITs, utilities. The thresholds, DCF variants, and qualitative overlays in this pack are calibrated for tech business models — they will produce confidently-wrong outputs on non-tech names.

Specifically, the pack will not be extended to support:

- **Auto OEMs (GM, F, STLA, TM)** — capex-heavy, financing-arm complexity, cycle-normalised earnings required.
- **Banks and insurers** — book-value / regulatory-capital frameworks, not DCF.
- **Pure cyclicals (chemicals, steel, mining)** — through-cycle EBITDA frameworks required.
- **Yield-track equities** (utilities, REITs, telcos) — dividend-discount frameworks; the YIELD track is dormant by design.

If you want to evaluate a non-tech name, do it outside this pack — the skills will run, but the verdicts won't be trustworthy. Known failure modes for non-tech inputs:

- Signal's P/S and PEG thresholds are tech-calibrated; P/S < 0.5 reads as BUY but is normal for asset-heavy industries.
- Model's two-stage DCF breaks when the trailing FCF series contains negative years (common for cyclicals and EV-transitioning autos).
- Model's pre-profit variant assumes SBC-driven dilution; it does not handle buyback-heavy share-count trajectories.
- AI layer classification has no analogue for non-tech structural advantages (brand, scale, regulatory moat).

---

## Skill order

```
/stock-screen TICKER1, TICKER2, …     fast PASS / WATCH / SKIP across a list
        │
        ▼
/stock-signal TICKER                  GARP verdict + MODEL_READY gate (single ticker)
        │
        ▼
/stock-timing TICKER                  when-to-act overlay (SUE, PEAD, revisions, catalyst)
        │
        ▼
/stock-model TICKER                   DCF intrinsic value range + position sizing
```

All four merge into one `reports/TICKER_YYYYMMDD.json` (stages: `screen`, `signal`, `timing`, `model`). Model is hard-gated on a same-session Signal. Timing is an overlay — only meaningful when Signal or Model is BUY/WATCH. Screen and Signal are independent entry points; you can start at either.

`/stock-equity` (router, planned) orchestrates the full chain or dispatches to sub-skills based on user phrasing.

---

## How `/stock-model` works (and when it refuses)

Model is a valuation calculator that estimates "what is one share actually worth today?" It projects free cash flow 5 years out, adds a terminal value for everything after that, discounts both back to present using a cost-of-capital number, subtracts net debt, and divides by share count. It runs the math three times — bear / base / bull — to produce a range, not a single point.

**The hard requirement.** Model only runs when Signal upstream says `MODEL_READY = YES` (or `CONDITIONAL` with `--confirm`). The gate is deliberate: Signal is the one place that strips stock-based comp from EPS, classifies profit stage, and decides whether a DCF is honest right now. If Signal says NO, Model refuses — `--confirm` does not override a NO.

**The three states Signal can issue:**

| State | What it means | What Model does |
|---|---|---|
| `YES` | Profitable, clean EPS positive, no qualitative red flags | Runs standard two-stage DCF |
| `CONDITIONAL` | One named risk the user must acknowledge (e.g. transition-year ticker) | Refuses until re-invoked with `--confirm` |
| `NO` | Clean EPS negative, qualitative FAIL, or hard CAUTION | Hard refusal; re-run `/signal` if conditions changed |

**Why the standard DCF needs profitability.** The two-stage model assumes a terminal value where earnings ≈ free cash flow in steady state. For a company that pays out more in stock comp than it earns in GAAP profit (e.g. XYZ/Block today), the steady-state assumption is a fiction — small changes to the perpetuity growth rate flip the answer by orders of magnitude. Forcing the calculator on these inputs produces a confident-looking number anchored to nothing.

**The pre-profit variant (`--pre-profit`).** For EMERGING tickers (or any ticker the user forces with `--pre-profit`), Model swaps the Gordon terminal for a revenue-multiple exit and adds an explicit SBC dilution schedule plus an FCF inflection year. This is the right shape for high-growth tech that hasn't reached steady state yet. **It still requires the gate to pass** — pre-profit is a math change, not a gate bypass.

**Common reasons Signal will issue NO:**

- Clean EPS negative after SBC stripping (e.g. heavy stock-comp tech that's GAAP break-even)
- Qualitative FAIL from a TAM ceiling or governance issue
- Pre-profit base effect (EMERGING tickers always default to CAUTION)
- PEG > 2.0 with no overriding qualitative strength

**The fix when Signal says NO is almost never "force Model to run."** It's either:

1. Re-run `/signal TICKER` after using the Manual Input Protocol to supply a missing input (e.g. analyst 5y EPS growth if yfinance returned null), or
2. Acknowledge that the standard DCF isn't the right tool for this company today — and use Signal's qualitative output + Timing for the entry/exit decision instead.

**TL;DR.** Model is a valuation calculator for *profitable, stable tech*. It works cleanly on mature compounders (MSFT, GOOGL, ADBE). It refuses — correctly — on pre-profit names and on profitable-but-SBC-absorbed names where the inputs would lie. The pre-profit variant extends the range but doesn't remove the upstream gate.

---

## Build tools by component

| Component | Build with |
|-----------|-----------|
| yfinance MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
| EDGAR MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
| `/stock-signal`, `/stock-screen`, `/stock-timing`, `/stock-model`, `/stock-equity` skills | `skill-creator:skill-creator` |
| Report UI (Vite + React) | `agent-skills:build` |

---

## Run

```bash
# UI
cd ui && npm run dev        # localhost:5173
cd ui && npm run build

# MCP servers
cd mcp/yf && python server.py
cd mcp/edgar && python server.py
```

Tasks and milestones live in Linear: https://linear.app/ababushkin/project/ai-equity-research-skill-pack-b8446cbaab6b/overview

content.text:
README updated. Changes:

- **Title** fixed: `stock-review` → `stock-screener` (matches the repo and plugin name).
- **New "Dependencies" section** — a table covering Claude Code, `uv` (with the no-venv explanation), Python ≥3.11, and Node/npm (UI only), plus the per-server package lists and the GUI-PATH caveat.
- **New "Installation" section** — the `/plugin marketplace add` → `/plugin install` → `/mcp` verify flow, with a pointer to `INSTALL.md` for the dev/symlink path.
- **"Run" section** refreshed — the stale `python server.py` (venv) commands are now `uv run --script ...`, noting they're for standalone debugging since Claude Code normally launches them via `.mcp.json`.

That rounds out the packaging work: plugin scaffolding, `uv` MCP migration, `INSTALL.md`, and now the README all line up. Working tree now also has `M README.md` alongside the earlier plugin changes — all ready for you to commit when you want.

I'll commit on `main` — this repo's history shows direct-to-main commits (the recent `feat`/`chore`/`fix` log), and that matches "commit and push" here. I'll split it into two logical commits (the packaging feature, then the test-artifact reports), then push.
